questions.exp: Integers are immutable. y = x makes y point to 10. y = 20 rebinds y to a new object. x still points to 10.
breakdown: y = x → both y and x point to int object 10
y = 20 → y now points to new int object 20

questions.exp: b = a does NOT copy the list. Both a and b reference the same list object. Appending to b also changes what a sees.
breakdown: a = [1,2,3] → list object created in memory
b = a → b is an alias, points to same list

questions.exp: y = x[:] creates a shallow copy — a new list. Mutating y does not affect x.
breakdown: x[:] creates a new list object with same elements
y and x are now separate objects

questions.exp: Shallow copy duplicates the outer list but inner objects are shared. b[1] and a[1] point to the same inner list [2,3].
breakdown: a[:] creates a new outer list
Inner [2,3] is still shared between a[1] and b[1]
b[1].append(99) mutates the shared inner list

questions.code:
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [1, 2, 3]
print(a == b)
print(a is b)
questions.exp: == checks value equality (both have same elements → True). 'is' checks identity (different objects in memory → False).
breakdown: a and b are separate list objects with equal values
Two separate list literals always create two objects

questions.code:
a = 256
b = 256
print(a is b)

x = 257
y = 257
print(x is y)
questions.exp: Python caches small integers from -5 to 256. 256 reuses the cached object → True. 257 is outside the cache → two new objects → False.
breakdown: a and b both point to the cached 256 object
x and y are above 256, new objects created → x is y → False

questions.code:
x = None
print(x == None)
print(x is None)
questions.exp: None is a singleton — there is only ONE None object. Both == and 'is' return True. Best practice is to use 'is None'.
breakdown: None is a singleton object in Python
x is None → True (same singleton object)
PEP 8 recommends using 'is None' not '== None'

questions.exp: Tuples support indexing. t[0] accesses the first element which is 1.
Tuples are immutable. Attempting to assign to an index raises TypeError: 'tuple' object does not support item assignment.
breakdown: Tuples cannot be modified after creation
Use a list if you need mutability

questions.exp: The tuple itself is immutable (can't reassign t[1]), but it contains a mutable list. Mutating the list in-place is allowed.
breakdown: We're not reassigning t[1], just mutating the list it points to
Tuple's reference to the list is unchanged → (1, [2,3,99], 4)

questions.exp: Strings are zero-indexed. s[0]='h', s[1]='e', s[2]='l'...
Negative indexing counts from the end. s[-1] is the last character 'n'.
s[::2] slices with step=2, starting at 0. Picks indices 0,2,4 → 'a','c','e' → 'ace'.
breakdown: s[::2] = start default, stop default, step 2

questions.exp: 'hello' * 2 = 'hellohello'. Concatenating '!' gives 'hellohello!'.
Strings are immutable. You cannot change a character in place. This raises TypeError.
breakdown: Raises TypeError: 'str' object does not support item assignment
Use s = 'H' + s[1:] to create a new string

questions.exp: append() adds the entire [4,5] as ONE element. The list becomes [1,2,3,[4,5]] — 4 elements.
breakdown: [4,5] is added as a single nested list

questions.exp: extend() adds each element individually. [4,5] adds 2 elements. lst becomes [1,2,3,4,5] → len = 5.
sorted() returns a NEW sorted list, leaving the original unchanged. lst.sort() would modify in-place.
breakdown: sorted(lst) creates a new sorted list

questions.exp: b = a makes b reference [1,2,3]. a = [4,5,6] REBINDS a to a new list. b still references the original [1,2,3].
breakdown: a = [4,5,6] → a now points to a new list

questions.exp: Slicing [1:4] includes indices 1, 2, 3 (stop=4 is exclusive). Elements at those indices: 2, 3, 4.
dict.get(key, default) returns the default value when the key doesn't exist. 'c' is not in d, so 0 is returned.
Duplicate keys are not allowed. The later value overwrites the earlier one. 'a': 3 overwrites 'a': 1.
Adding 'y':20, then deleting 'x' leaves only {'y': 20}.
setdefault returns the existing value if key exists. First call creates 'a':[] and appends 1. Second call returns [1] and appends 2. Result: {'a':[1,2]}.
breakdown: First setdefault: 'a' absent → sets to [] → returns []

questions.exp: A bare return (or no return) returns None. print(None) outputs None.
Inside fn(), x=20 creates a LOCAL variable. The global x=10 is unaffected. fn() prints 20, outer print(x) prints 10.

questions.code:
def fn(lst=[]):
    lst.append(1)
    return lst
print(fn())
print(fn())
questions.exp: Mutable default arguments persist between calls. The same list is reused. First call appends 1 → [1]. Second call appends again → [1,1].
breakdown: Default list [] is created ONCE at function definition

questions.exp: This is a closure. inner() captures x from outer's scope. Even after outer() returns, fn() (which is inner) remembers x='hello'.
breakdown: outer() runs, sets x='hello', returns inner function
fn = outer() → fn is the inner function
fn() calls inner(), which accesses captured x

questions.exp: *args collects all positional arguments into a tuple (1,2,3,4). sum((1,2,3,4)) = 10.
The lambda multiplies x by 2. f(5) = 5 * 2 = 10.

questions.code:
fns = [lambda x: x+i for i in range(3)]
print(fns[0](10))
questions.exp: Classic closure gotcha. All lambdas share the SAME variable i. By the time any is called, i=2 (end of loop). fns[0](10) = 10+2 = 12.
breakdown: lambda captures i by reference, not by value
All three lambdas now use i=2

questions.code:
fns = [lambda x, i=i: x+i for i in range(3)]
print(fns[0](10))
questions.exp: Using default argument i=i captures the current value of i at creation time. fns[0] was created with i=0. fns[0](10) = 10+0 = 10.
breakdown: i=i as default arg captures current i at creation
fns[0] created when i=0 → i=0 captured
fns[1] created when i=1 → i=1 captured

questions.code:
for i in range(3):
    print(i, end=' ')
questions.exp: range(3) produces 0,1,2. end=' ' replaces the newline with a space. Output: '0 1 2 ' (trailing space).
breakdown: Each iteration prints i followed by space

questions.exp: The else clause runs only if the loop completed WITHOUT break. Since break fires at i=3, else is skipped. Only 'end' is printed.
continue skips even numbers. Only odd numbers (1,3,5) are added. 1+3+5 = 9.
When v==3 (i=2), lst.pop(2) removes 3. The list shifts. Modifying a list while iterating can cause issues but here the result is [1,2,4,5].
breakdown: Iteration jumps — 4 is skipped in check but result is [1,2,4,5]

questions.code:
result = [x**2 for x in range(5)]
print(result)

d = {x: x**2 for x in range(4)}
print(d[3])
questions.exp: Dict comprehension creates {0:0, 1:1, 2:4, 3:9}. d[3] = 9.

questions.code:
flat = [x for row in [[1,2],[3,4],[5]] for x in row]
print(flat)
questions.exp: Nested list comprehension flattens. Outer loop iterates rows, inner loop iterates each element in the row.

questions.code:
class A:
    x = 5
a1 = A()
a2 = A()
a1.x = 10
print(a1.x, a2.x, A.x)
questions.exp: a1.x = 10 creates an INSTANCE attribute on a1, shadowing the class attribute. a2 and A still use the class attribute x=5.
breakdown: A.x = 5 is a class attribute shared by all instances
a1.x = 10 creates a NEW instance attribute on a1

questions.exp: C doesn't define fn(), so MRO searches up. C inherits from A (not B), so A.fn() is found and called → 'A'.
breakdown: C inherits from A, not B
C has no fn() of its own

questions.code:
class Counter:
    count = 0
    def __init__(self):
        Counter.count += 1

a = Counter()
b = Counter()
print(Counter.count)
questions.exp: count is a class attribute. Each __init__ call increments Counter.count. Two instances created → count = 2.
ZeroDivisionError is caught → 'caught' prints. finally ALWAYS runs → 'finally' prints.
int('abc') raises ValueError (invalid literal). The first except clause catches it. else runs only on success — not called here.
breakdown: int('abc') → ValueError (type is OK, value is wrong)

questions.exp: Exponentiation is RIGHT-associative. 3**2=9 is evaluated first, then 2**9=512.
bool is a subclass of int. True=1, False=0. 1 + 1 + 0 = 2.
Python supports chained comparisons. x > 3 > 1 means (x > 3) and (3 > 1) = True and True = True.
not True = False. not False = True. not True = False. Three 'not' operations on True → False.
Python sees x = 'local' inside fn() and treats x as local throughout the function. Accessing x before assignment raises UnboundLocalError.
breakdown: Python analyzes function body at compile time
x = 'local' makes x a local variable
print(x) happens BEFORE x is assigned → UnboundLocalError
Use 'global x' to refer to the global x

questions.code:
def outer():
    x = 10
    def inner():
        nonlocal x
        x = 20
    inner()
    print(x)
outer()
questions.exp: nonlocal x refers to outer's x. inner() modifies it to 20. After inner() returns, outer's x is 20.
breakdown: nonlocal x → refers to outer's x
inner() sets x = 20 in outer's scope

questions.exp: map(str, [1,2,3]) applies str() to each element. 1→'1', 2→'2', 3→'3'. list() converts the map object.
zip() stops at the shortest iterable. b has 2 elements, so only 2 pairs are produced.

questions.code:
def gen():
    yield 1
    yield 2
    yield 3

g = gen()
print(next(g))
print(next(g))
questions.exp: next(g) resumes the generator from where it left off. First next → yields 1. Second next → yields 2.
breakdown: Second next(g) → resumes, runs to 'yield 2' → returns 2

questions.code:
def gen():
    yield 1
    yield 2

g = gen()
list(g)
print(list(g))
questions.exp: Generators can only be iterated ONCE. list(g) exhausts it. The second list(g) starts iteration on an exhausted generator → empty list.
breakdown: Second list(g) → generator has no more values

questions.exp: type([]) is <class 'list'> and type(()) is <class 'tuple'>. They are different types, so == is False.
For lists, += calls __iadd__ which extends the list IN-PLACE. y and x point to the same list. y sees [1,2,3,4]. (Note: += on strings/tuples creates a new object.)
breakdown: y = x → both reference same list

questions.exp: Floating point arithmetic is imprecise. 0.1+0.2 = 0.30000000000000004, not exactly 0.3. Use math.isclose() for float comparisons.
breakdown: 0.1 and 0.2 have no exact binary representation

questions.exp: Python interns (caches) short string literals. Both a and b reference the same interned string object → is returns True. (Not guaranteed in all cases.)
breakdown: a and b point to same object
